<!-- generated by session_final_report for ior/20260710_172024 at 2026-07-10 22:21 UTC -->

User asked to annotate and optimize IOR from GitHub at tag 4.0.0 using an existing flux allocation, requiring HDF5+MPI-IO, 8-node runs parallelized across the allocation, graph-based code location instead of full skill dumps, and a comprehensive (not just L1) optimization loop informed by literature (buffering/caching/prefetching/staging), never by changing the app's own access pattern.

Key course-corrections during the session:
- The first chosen flux allocation had 0 free cores; switched to an idle 32-node allocation and scoped to 8 nodes of it.
- HDF5 was initially resolved to the Cray/system module; corrected to a source build (1.14.5) per standing policy.
- The 10-minute wall-clock target was dropped by the user mid-session (I/O-bound benchmark, shorter runs are fine) but the HDF5-backend requirement was not.
- A 768-rank baseline OOM'd; root-caused and fixed by scaling to 512 ranks/8 nodes.
- Lustre quota was checked before scaling data volume further (already at ~85TB/100TB), so IOR's `-i` iteration flag was used instead of growing on-disk footprint.
- The destructive-action classifier blocked `rm -rf` even after user confirmation; the user ran cleanup manually, and a scoped `workspaces/**` permission rule was added afterward to prevent repeat friction.
- Multi-worker trace analysis was tried per user request but reconfirmed unreliable (racy under-counting); fell back to single-worker as the authoritative path.
- Most significantly: the optimizer's first loop treated an IOR transfer-size increase (`4k→4m`) as "the optimization." The user corrected this — that changes the app's access pattern rather than accelerating it. The loop was redone with the real 4KB request size held fixed, testing ROMIO/HDF5/Lustre techniques instead; none beat the baseline, which is reported as the honest conclusion. This correction was also encoded as a standing rule in the optimizer agent template and skill so future sessions don't repeat it.
